etymology.description: 功(功) (gōng, "achievement; work") + 夫(夫) (fū, "man; husband") = skill developed through work -> martial arts
etymology.interpretation: 功(功) (gōng, "achievement") refers to the effort and work one puts into mastering a particular skill. 夫(夫) (fū, "man") can imply the idea of a person or practitioner. "功夫" (gōngfu) therefore signifies the result of hard work and persistent practice leading to skill and expertise. While often associated with martial arts, these components initially suggested any skill or merit achieved through time and effort. Over time, it became most closely associated with Chinese martial arts, hence 'kung fu'.

examples.chinese: 学习功夫需要很多时间(學習功夫需要很多時間)。
examples.pinyin: Xuéxí gōngfu xūyào hěn duō shíjiān.
examples.english: Learning kung fu requires a lot of time.

examples.chinese: 他的功夫很厉害(他的功夫很厲害)。
examples.english: His kung fu is very impressive.

examples.chinese: 我们可以在公园里练功夫(我們可以在公園裡練功夫)。
examples.pinyin: Wǒmen kěyǐ zài gōngyuán lǐ liàn gōngfu.
examples.english: We can practice kung fu in the park.
